paragraphs.body: In the article, "Ask Ethan: How Can We See 46.1 billion Light-Years Away in A 13.8- BillionYear-Old Universe? ", Ethan Siegel supports the question, If the Universe began 13.8 billion years ago, how can we see objects more than 46.1 Lights years away. He emphasizes this by developing several essential ideas. First, Siegel declares that the Universe is rapidly expanding. The Big Bang is the cause of the Universe's expansion. The massive explosion over 13.8 billion years ago has expanded the Universe to approximately 93 billion light years. Finally, Siegel informs us that redshift light lets us see beyond 13.8 billion years. An object emits the redshifted light when it is moving away from Earth. Using this information, we can estimate how fast an object moves away from us. Therefore, if we see an object's light from 13 billion years ago and it redshifted, we know it is now several light years further away from Earth than perceived.

annotations.text: Therefore, if we see an object's light from 13 billion years ago and it redshifted,

paragraphs.body: In high school I would say my personality developed a lot, many people would say that I am a completely different person now. For instance, I was very shy and did not put myself out there. I had my niche, and I was okay with who I was back then. I kept to myself most of the time, it was like I was just living in my own world on my own schedule. My senior year I started to come out of my shell, I was hanging out with my friends more often and going out of my comfort zone in many ways. I would still say that I kept to myself, but not in a closed off and shy way.

annotations.text: In high school I would say my personality developed a lot, many people would say that I am a completely different person now.
annotations.comment: I'm very interested in this shift, but it feels logically disconnected from your first paragraph. Can you transition, or bridge the two paragraphs with a line or idea or two?

annotations.context_left: most of the time, it was like I was just living in my own world on my own schedule. My

paragraphs.body: My group of friends were inseparable in high school. We did Everything together; we played all the same sports in high school, and all four got the same job and worked together. However, one inevitable thing was coming that would jeopardize our relationship with one another, and that was graduation. All four of our orbits were taking us on different paths. Dylan's path took him to a college 3 hours away, where he is consumed with Football and class; Boulder's path took him to an electrician apprenticeship right outside of our hometown; Carter's path took him to senior year of High School, and my path took me 11 hours away from our hometown to the University of Pittsburgh.

annotations.text: All four of our orbits were taking us on different paths.
annotations.comment: Aha! A space reference--so your essay is developing a metaphor.

paragraphs.body: In months, we went from Hanging out every day to calling every week to now calling every month. As our lives expanded, we slowly drifted apart from each other. The communication between the two slowly redshifted after each call. According to Seigel, "We discovered that the spiral and elliptical nebulae in the sky were galaxies beyond our own; we measured the distance to them; we discovered that the farther away they were, the greater their light was redshifted." In other words, Siegel is saying that the further away two objects are from one another, the more their light redshifts, similarly to the communication between me and my friends. With each conversation, we slowly have less and less to talk about with each other. The words coming through the phone did not sound as they did three months ago.

annotations.text: two slowly redshifted after each call.
annotations.comment: This is interesting! But if you're going to rely on this metaphor, you probably need to develop the metaphor. Perhaps you need a paragraph between your essay's 1st and 2nd paragraphs to define that metaphor for your reader.
Nice voice and tone in this essay :)

paragraphs.body: Even though relationships constantly evolve and change, they do not always have to be for the worst. In some relationships, you can take the same path, gravitate toward each other, orbiting the same object. This is the course of action my girlfriend Kayley and I took, although the road was not always paved. We started dating when she was a senior in high school, and I was a junior. This meant that the same inevitable graduation event was bound to rip us apart. The worst part was that I knew exactly where she was going and how far away it was. The only sliver of hope I had left was that her parents' house was still in my hometown. That was until they decided to sell their house and permanently move.

annotations.comment: be careful not to mix metaphors!

annotations.text: gravitate toward each other, orbiting the same object.

paragraphs.body: The day she left for college honestly felt like the ending of a sad movie. Her parents made it so that when she left for college, they would leave the house in my hometown and could drive together. Being the good person I am, I came over that day and helped her get packed for college and helped her parents move out of the house. When we finished packing, we said our goodbyes, not knowing what the future had in store. We got in our separate cars and took off. We took a similar route and were side by side at a red light. She was in the turning lane, and I was going straight. She rolled down her window and blew me a kiss as the light turned green, and we went our separate ways. In that same car ride, she bought a plane ticket to come back to see me on the first break she got.

Even though she was over 1000 miles (about the distance from Georga to Pennsylvania) away, our communication over the phone was not redshifted. In his Forbes article, Ask Ethan: How Can We See 46.1 billion Light-Years Away In A 13.8-Billion-Year-Old Universe? Ethan Siegel explains scientists can measure how far objects are away from earth based on their light. According to Seigel, "The light that we perceive will be redshifted or blueshifted dependent on our relative velocity to the object we are observing, and the light-travel time will not necessarily be the same as the actual present-day distance between any two objects." Basically, Siegel says that if the object's velocity moves toward us rather than away the light will be blueshifted. The connection we had with each other felt blueshifted. Our relationship was flourishing rather than diminishing; the moment I realized this was when I committed to the University of Pittsburgh.

annotations.text: The connection we had with each other felt blueshifted. Our relationship was flourishing rather than diminishing; the moment I realized this was when I committed to the University of Pittsburgh.
annotations.comment: This is interesting! I like the way you're exploring how relationships either blue or redshift as you grow. I wonder, though, what the "so what" of this observation is? Who is your audience, specifically, for this essay?

paragraphs.body: I would say that since starting my life here in Pittsburgh, my personality has developed a little further, I am still a little introverted, although I really had to get out of my comfort zone to meet new people in a place, where I only had one person in my corner. I started initiating conversations with people and making new friends. Although I have only been living here for a couple of months, I can already see my personality flourishing under pressure. I hope for the future, that I can continue to grow my personality. In the future, I see myself having the same friends that I had in the past, but I also see myself with new friends, and a personality that many people want to be around. Coming to college so far away from home has given me a clean state for my future. I am in control of who I want to become, and that is a better man. For the people around me, my family back home, and for my future family, one where I will be a husband and a father.

annotations.text: I am in control of who I want to become, and that is a better man. For the people around me, my family back home, and for my future family, one where I will be a husband and a father.
annotations.comment: How might this be related to redshifting or blueshifting?

paragraphs.body: In conclusion, relationships are always changing, but the one thing you can control is who you are. Although that is encoded around relationships, it is not everything. It is up to you to decide who you are, and who you want to become in the future. Sometimes you must step out of your comfort zone and test yourself to see who you really are and compare that with the person you want to become. You cannot control the past, but you are in control of the present which is the building block for your future.

annotations.text: Sometimes you must step out of your comfort zone and test yourself to see who you really are and compare that with the person you want to become. You cannot control the past, but you are in control of the present which is the building block for your future.
annotations.comment: I wonder if there's something in the metaphor of redshifting that could reveal, more clearly, the central focusing idea of this essay?
